CLAYSON, ROTHROCK & MANN

ATTORNEYS AT LAW 
CLAYSON LAW BUILDING 
60, SOUTH MAl N STREET 
POST OFFICE BOX 1447 
CORONA, CALiFORNIA 91720 
November 19, 1980 
Ms. Barbara A. Milman, Counsel 
Legal Division 
Fair Political Practices Commission 
P.O. Box 807 
Sacramento, CA 
95804 
RE: 
Palo Verde Irrigation District 
Possible Conflict of Interest 
Dear Ms. Milman: 
AREA CODE 7 
737~19iO 
689~7241 
The President and Board of Trustees of the Palo Verde 
Irrigation District asked me to respond to your letter of 
October 24, 1980 with regard to a possible conflict of 
interest problem in setting the 1980-1981 water tolls. 
Palo Verde Irrigation District's Act (Water Code Appendix 
Section 33-10a) provides that the Board of Trustees of the 
district in lieu of levying taxes may fix rates of toll and 
charges for irrigation and other public uses and collect them 
from all persons using water for irrigation and/or from all 
persons owning or possessing land within the district which 
may be 
entitled to water for irrigation. 
It provides that 
if the tolls and charges are fixed and levied upon any basis 
other than in accordance with the use of water, the Board of 
Trustees will hold a hearing upon the manner, rate and amount 
of such tolls and changes. 
Not all of the land in the Palo Verde Irrigation District 
uses irrigation water, but members of the Board of Trustees 
are required to be owners of real property within the district 
(Water Code Appendix Section 33-5). 
As a result, the question 
of whether or not to levy a water toll and how much of a water 
toll to levy involves a conflict of interest. 
Under the PVID 
act, all of the revenue required to operate the district could 
be raised through taxes. 
Historically, virtually all of the 
trustees have paid water tolls although there have occasionally 


Ms. Barbara A. Milman 
November 19, 1980 
Page 2 
been members of the Board who did not. 
Revenues of the 
district have been raised primarily from water tolls and 
taxes have been a minor part of the total revenues. 
Accordingly, there is a conflict of interest on whether 
or not any water toll should be levied and, if a water toll 
is levied, how much it should be. 
In addition, there is a 
conflict of interest as to what land should pay water tolls 
and whether or not the water tolls should be different for 
different lands. 
Because every member of the Board of 
Trustees is required to own land in the district, every 
member of the Board of Trustees has a conflict of interest 
on all water toll matters. 
Most of the land in the Palo Verde Valley can obtain 
gravity flow water from the established canals. 
Some of the 
land in the district is above the canals and some of the land 
is so close to the level of the canals that pumping is required 
to efficiently irrigate the land. 
This raises the cost of 
irrigating those lands to which water is being pumped. 
In 
some cases, pumping is actually necessary, in other cases it 
improves efficiency. 
In the past the district has paid for pumping the water 
to some lands as part of an agreement relating to relocation 
of canals. 
In the past all lands have been charged the same 
water toll, whether or not pumping was required, and whether 
or not pumping 
was furnished by the district. 
Lands which 
required pumping were normally assessed at lower values than 
lands which did not require pumping. 
When water tolls were relatively small and the total 
operational costs of the district were relatively small, the 
cost of pumping was not a major item and it was at least, 
in part, offset by the lower assessed value. 
The greatly 
increased cost of energy and the fact that most of the 
district's expenses are borne by the water tolls has made the 
tax adjustment somewhat meaningless. 
In this context a motion was made at the March 18, 1980 
meeting to give a $5.00 per acre credit against the water toll 
to those lands which were pumping water which pumping was not 
already paid for by the PVID. 
I understood the motion to be 
a proposal to change the basis for levying the water toll which 
could not be accomplished without following the Code Section 
relating to advertising, hearing and adopting the water toll 
(Water Code Appendix Section 233-l0a). 
The PVID Conflict of 


Ms. Barbara A. Milman 
November 19, 1980 
Page 3 
Interest Code does not disqualify a trustee from voting to 
"fix a uniform water toll" by reason of "investment, interest 
in real property .•• " et cetera (Section D-2). 
Since the PVID 
Act requires that a notice be published and a hearing be held 
in the event the water toll is to be set on any basis other 
than accordance with the use of water, it is necessary to 
decide in advance what the basis for the water toll will be. 
I, therefore, regarded the action being taken at the March 18, 
1980 meeting as a part of the process to "fix a uniform water 
toll" and, therefore, owning land which pays water tolls was 
not a disqualifying interest. 
Even in the absence of the "uniform water toll" exemption, 
it would be my opinion that a trustee is not disqualified from 
voting on water tolls just because he owns land that pays water 
tolls. 
If that were true, only those persons not owning land, 
and therefore not financially affected by setting the water 
tolls, could vote on setting water tolls. 
Since every trustee 
is required to own land there would be no trustee without a 
conflict of interest. 
If some land in the district gets its 
water toll reduced or removed, it pays less proportionately 
and all other lands pay more proportionately, so all landowners 
who pay water tolls have a financial interest in what lands pay 
water tolls and how much they pay. 
All persons who own land 
which do not pay water tOlls have a similar interest. 
One of the trustees, not the one who made the motion, 
disqualified himself because he owns land to which water is 
being pumped and which would qualify for the $5.00 reduction 
in water toll. 
In my opinion he was not disqualified to vote 
because setting the basis of the water toll is a part of the 
process of fixing the water toll. 
Accordingly, when there was 
a tie vote without him I advised him he was entitled to vote. 
He voted in favor of advertising the water toll with the $5.00 
reduction. 
The word "uniform" was inserted in the PVID Conflicts of 
Interest Code at the request of prior counsel for the Fair 
Political Practices Commission, 
after I had explained to him 
in great detail the very problems that have now arisen. 
In my 
opinion the word "uniform" should be interpreted to mean "of 
general application" and the water toll is "uniform" if it 
applies equally to everyone who owns property of a particular 
kind. 
It should be noted that some lands in the district pay 
no water tolls at all. 
If the particular trustee were dis-
qualified from voting because he has an interest in land which 
would pay a lower water toll, it would seem to follow that any 


Ms. Barbara A. Milman 
November 19, 1980 
Page 4 
trustee who owns land which pays no water toll would also be 
disqualified, and a trustee which has no land which would 
receive the reduced rate, but who does not pay water tolls 
on other land, also has a "financial interest" in the decision. 
Since the water toll cannot be set upon any basis other 
than the use of water without notice of public hearing, the 
motion on March 18 amounted to a proposal as to how the notice 
of the fixing of the water toll should be written when it was 
published. 
The result of the action on March 18 at which the trustees 
voted was that the advertised notice of the proposed water toll 
provided that the Board of Trustees "proposed to fix the water 
toll at $22.50 per acre, or fraction thereof, for all land 
too high to receive gravity flow of water from the existing 
canal system, and to which water is being applied by pumping 
the cost of which pumping is not paid for by the district, and 
$27.50 per acre, or fraction thereof, for all other lands 
feasible for agriculture within the district, regardless of 
whether said land is planted in crop and using water or not." 
Without the motion the words "$22.50 per acre, or fraction 
thereof, for all lands too high to receive gravity flow water 
from the existing canal system and to which water is being 
applied by pumping, the cost of which pumping is not paid for 
by the district and" would not have been included in the notice 
and could not have been fixed as the water toll in the absence 
of another advertised notice and hearing. 
The vote taken on 
March 18, 1980 had no immediate effect except on internal 
district procedures. 
The adoption of the proposed water toll was advertised 
as required in the act and came up for hearing on May 19, 1980. 
At that time no one appeared to protest the proposed water 
toll and no written protests were received, and on May 19, 1980, 
the water toll was adopted, all trustees voting in favor of it. 
As I see it, the financial interests of every member of 
the Board of Trustees was affected. 
A trustee who owns land which paid no water tolls would 
have had his tax burden greatly increased if the water toll 
had not been levied. 
Whether or not a $5.00 difference in 
water tolls between the pumped land and gravity flow land 
would adversely affect him would depend on whether the burden 
was passed to the gravity flow water tolls or to the taxes. 


• 
j 
, 
Ms. Barbara A. Milman 
November 19, 1980 
Page 5 
A trustee who owns gravity flow lands was directly 
interested because to the extent the expenses of the district 
were raised by water tolls and not by taxes, the relative 
burden on gravity flow lands would be increased. 
Finally, the owner of land receiving pumped water, which 
would have a reduced water toll, had a conflict because the 
reduced water toll credit would be beneficial to him. 
There is also an overlying problem which involves the 
relative proportion of each type of land owned by the trustee 
within the district. 
That is, a trustee may own some of each 
type of land. 
That was probably true in the case of the 
trustee who initially disqualified himself. 
Although we did 
not actually check on the number of acres, the result could be 
that even as to a trustee who owned land to which water was 
being pumped he might have enough land ownership receiving 
gravity flow water that the net effect on him would be to 
increase his water tolls. 
Whether or not this would be true 
would depend on the exact proportion of each type of land which 
he owns. 
As you can see, we have a rather complicated situation on 
our hands and it has been my opinion that all trustees are 
qualified to vote on matters affecting the water toll, because 
they are all required to own land in the district. 
As I 
indicated above, this was the topic of considerable discussion 
when we were adopting the original Conflicts of Interest Code 
for the district, and I sincerely hope the Fair Political 
Practices Commission will bear problems like this in mind 
trying to set a uniform Conflicts of Interest Code. 
Yours ver~ truly, 
RHM:slh 
cc: 
Paula Todd, PVID 


LAW OFFICES 
-JOSEPH WOOLDRIDGE" 
A. CAMERON PAULDEN-
ROBERT J. SELF" 
YOL"NG, WOOLDRIDGE, PAULDEN, SELF, FARR & HUGIE 
MICHAEL 
YOUNG-
ELDON R. hUGIE" 
CJAVID 
GRIF;:-I~" 
ERNEST A. CONA:"n 
PHILIP W. GANONG 
MICHAEL E. 
STEVE W. NICHOLS 
PROFESSIONAL CORPORAtiON 
Ms. Barbara A. Milman 
A PARTNERSHIP INCLUDING PROFESSIONAL CO;:;:PORATIONS 
1675 CHESTER AVENUE 
FOURTH 
FLOOR 
BAKERSFIELD, CALIFORNIA 93301 
April 22, 1981 
Staff Counsel, Legal Division 
Fair Political Practices Commission 
Post Office Box 807 
Sacramento, California 95804 
Re: 
Palo Verde Irrigation District 
Your Letter of January 22, 1981 
Our File: 
GF 6.2B 
Dear Ms. Milman: 
Aftr Z~ S 1,. 4H 
OF COUNSEL: 
JOHN 8. YOUNG 
ROBERT D. GARDNER 
ADMINISTRATIVE ASSISTANT 
Mr. William Kuhs has provided us with a copy of your letter of 
January 22, 1981 and his letter in response thereto, dated April 20, 
1981. 
Although our interest in this matter arises from our representa-
tion of several public districts involved in distribution of water 
for agricultural purposes, this letter is not written for, nor 
authorized by, any of our clients. 
This office concurs with the 
views expressed by Mr. Kuhs and we urge that his letter be taken 
into consideration should further action be taken on this matter. 
In addition to the matters discussed by Mr. Kuhs, the narrow 
interpretation adopted in your letter of January 22, 1981 appears to 
negate landowner/director action upon even a "uniform water toll." 
Tolls can only be extracted to pay district expenses. 
To the extent 
water tolls are not established, the expenses are met from revenues 
from the landowners, either raised by ad valorem or special benefit 
assessments. 
Since it is unlikely that all land in a district is of 
equal assessed value or has the same cost of service, it can be 
argued that the landowners "benefit unequally" from the revenues 
raised by equal water tolls. 
That is, the land with the highest 
assessed value or at the greater elevation will pay a lesser share 
of district costs if all costs are paid from a uniform water toll 
(i.e., single price toll). 
We find no reason why a landowner/director 
cannot act to establish a water toll that is to have uniform applica-
tion in accordance with the facts considered. 
ACP:la 
. C. PAULDEN 
cc: 
Mr. William C. Kuhs, Esq. 


WILLiAM C, KUHS 
-lAMES R. PARKER, .JR, 
GAY LYNNE NATHO 
REBECCA WISEMAN 
Re: 
LAW OFFICES OF 
WILLIAM C. KUHS 
1213 SEVENTEENTH STREET 
BAKERSFIELD, CALIFORNIA 
(BOS) 322-4004 
1 2 , 1981

sections.question_synthetic: Whether trustees of the Palo Verde Irrigation District have disqualifying conflicts of interest when voting on water toll matters, given that all trustees are required to own property in the district and would be financially affected by toll decisions.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: Document appears to be incoming request/correspondence rather than FPPC advice letter response. No formal QUESTION/CONCLUSION sections present. Document is truncated at end. Two separate letters visible - November 1980 and April 1981. Extracted information from November 1980 letter which contains the substantive analysis.

embedding.qa_text: QUESTION: Whether trustees of the Palo Verde Irrigation District have disqualifying conflicts of interest when voting on water toll matters, given that all trustees are required to own property in the district and would be financially affected by toll decisions.

embedding.summary: This is correspondence from attorneys representing the Palo Verde Irrigation District to FPPC counsel regarding potential conflicts of interest in setting water tolls. The attorneys argue that all board trustees have conflicts but are not disqualified from voting because the statute exempts "uniform" water tolls and because every trustee is required to own land in the district.
